evidence.claim: The conferring members being met, let their business be to frame a _continental charter_ , or Charter of the United Colonies; (answering to what is called the Magna Charta of England) fixing the number and manner of choosing members of Congress, members of Assembly, with their date of sitting, and drawing the line of business and jurisdiction between them: (Always remembering, that our strength is continental, not provincial:) Securing freedom and property to all men, and above all things, the free exercise of religion, according to the dictates of conscience; with such other matter as is necessary for a charter to contain.

candidate_claims.statement: The conferring members being met, let their business be to frame a _continental charter_ , or Charter of the United Colonies; (answering to what is called the Magna Charta of England) fixing the number and manner of choosing members of Congress, members of Assembly, with their date of sitting, and drawing the line of business and jurisdiction between them: (Always remembering, that our strength is continental, not provincial:) Securing freedom and property to all men, and above all things, the free exercise of religion, according to the dictates of conscience; with such other matter as is necessary for a charter to contain.

evidence.claim: Immediately after which, the said Conference to dissolve, and the bodies which shall be chosen comformable to the said charter, to be the legislators and governors of this continent for the time being: Whose peace and happiness, may God preserve, Amen.

candidate_claims.statement: Immediately after which, the said Conference to dissolve, and the bodies which shall be chosen comformable to the said charter, to be the legislators and governors of this continent for the time being: Whose peace and happiness, may God preserve, Amen.

evidence.claim: Should any body of men be hereafter delegated for this or some similar purpose, I offer them the following extracts from that wise observer on governments _Dragonetti_ .

candidate_claims.statement: Should any body of men be hereafter delegated for this or some similar purpose, I offer them the following extracts from that wise observer on governments _Dragonetti_ .

evidence.claim: “The science” says he “of the politician consists in fixing the true point of happiness and freedom.

candidate_claims.statement: “The science” says he “of the politician consists in fixing the true point of happiness and freedom.

evidence.claim: Those men would deserve the gratitude of ages, who should discover a mode of government that contained the greatest sum of individual happiness, with the least national expense.”

candidate_claims.statement: Those men would deserve the gratitude of ages, who should discover a mode of government that contained the greatest sum of individual happiness, with the least national expense.”

evidence.claim: But where says some is the King of America?

candidate_claims.statement: But where says some is the King of America?

evidence.claim: I’ll tell you Friend, he reigns above, and doth not make havoc of mankind like the Royal Brute of Britain.

candidate_claims.statement: I’ll tell you Friend, he reigns above, and doth not make havoc of mankind like the Royal Brute of Britain.

evidence.claim: Yet that we may not appear to be defective even in earthly honors, let a day be solemnly set apart for proclaiming the charter; let it be brought forth placed on the divine law, the word of God; let a crown be placed thereon, by which the world may know, that so far as we approve as monarchy, that in America _the law is King_ .

candidate_claims.statement: Yet that we may not appear to be defective even in earthly honors, let a day be solemnly set apart for proclaiming the charter; let it be brought forth placed on the divine law, the word of God; let a crown be placed thereon, by which the world may know, that so far as we approve as monarchy, that in America _the law is King_ .

evidence.claim: For as in absolute governments the King is law, so in free countries the law _ought_ to be King; and there ought to be no other.

candidate_claims.statement: For as in absolute governments the King is law, so in free countries the law _ought_ to be King; and there ought to be no other.

evidence.claim: But lest any ill use should afterwards arise, let the crown at the conclusion of the ceremony be demolished, and scattered among the people whose right it is.

candidate_claims.statement: But lest any ill use should afterwards arise, let the crown at the conclusion of the ceremony be demolished, and scattered among the people whose right it is.

evidence.claim: A government of our own is our natural right: And when a man seriously reflects on the precariousness of human affairs, he will become convinced, that it is infinitely wiser and safer, to form a constitution of our own in a cool deliberate manner, while we have it in our power, than to trust such an interesting event to time and chance.

candidate_claims.statement: A government of our own is our natural right: And when a man seriously reflects on the precariousness of human affairs, he will become convinced, that it is infinitely wiser and safer, to form a constitution of our own in a cool deliberate manner, while we have it in our power, than to trust such an interesting event to time and chance.

evidence.claim: If we omit it now, some, Massanello may hereafter arise, who laying hold of popular disquietudes, may collect together the desperate and discontented, and by assuming to themselves the powers of government, may sweep away the liberties of the continent like a deluge.

candidate_claims.statement: If we omit it now, some, Massanello may hereafter arise, who laying hold of popular disquietudes, may collect together the desperate and discontented, and by assuming to themselves the powers of government, may sweep away the liberties of the continent like a deluge.

evidence.claim: Should the government of America return again into the hands of Britain, the tottering situation of things, will be a temptation for some desperate adventurer to try his fortune; and in such a case, what relief can Britain give?

candidate_claims.statement: Should the government of America return again into the hands of Britain, the tottering situation of things, will be a temptation for some desperate adventurer to try his fortune; and in such a case, what relief can Britain give?

evidence.claim: Ere she could hear the news, the fatal business might be done; and ourselves suffering like the wretched Britons under the oppression of the Conqueror.

candidate_claims.statement: Ere she could hear the news, the fatal business might be done; and ourselves suffering like the wretched Britons under the oppression of the Conqueror.

evidence.claim: Ye that oppose independance now, ye know not what ye do; ye are opening a door to eternal tyranny, by keeping vacant the seat of government.

candidate_claims.statement: Ye that oppose independance now, ye know not what ye do; ye are opening a door to eternal tyranny, by keeping vacant the seat of government.

evidence.claim: There are thousands, and tens of thousands, who would think it glorious to expel from the continent, that barbarous and hellish power, which hath stirred up the Indians and Negroes to destroy us, the cruelty hath a double guilt, it is dealing brutally by us, and treacherously by them.

candidate_claims.statement: There are thousands, and tens of thousands, who would think it glorious to expel from the continent, that barbarous and hellish power, which hath stirred up the Indians and Negroes to destroy us, the cruelty hath a double guilt, it is dealing brutally by us, and treacherously by them.

evidence.claim: To talk of friendship with those in whom our reason forbids us to have faith, and our affections wounded through a thousand pores instruct us to detest, is madness and folly.

candidate_claims.statement: To talk of friendship with those in whom our reason forbids us to have faith, and our affections wounded through a thousand pores instruct us to detest, is madness and folly.

evidence.claim: Every day wears out the little remains of kindred between us and them, and can there be any reason to hope, that as the relationship expires, the affection will increase, or that we shall agree better, when we have ten times more and greater concerns to quarrel over than ever?

candidate_claims.statement: Every day wears out the little remains of kindred between us and them, and can there be any reason to hope, that as the relationship expires, the affection will increase, or that we shall agree better, when we have ten times more and greater concerns to quarrel over than ever?

evidence.claim: Ye that tell us of harmony and reconciliation, can ye restore to us the time that is past?

candidate_claims.statement: Ye that tell us of harmony and reconciliation, can ye restore to us the time that is past?

evidence.claim: Can ye give to prostitution its former innocence?

candidate_claims.statement: Can ye give to prostitution its former innocence?

evidence.claim: Neither can ye reconcile Britain and America.

candidate_claims.statement: Neither can ye reconcile Britain and America.

evidence.claim: last cord now is broken, the people of England are presenting addresses against us.

candidate_claims.statement: last cord now is broken, the people of England are presenting addresses against us.

evidence.claim: There are injuries which nature cannot forgive; she would cease to be nature if she did.

candidate_claims.statement: There are injuries which nature cannot forgive; she would cease to be nature if she did.

evidence.claim: As well can the lover forgive the ravisher of his mistress, as the continent forgive the murders of Britain.

candidate_claims.statement: As well can the lover forgive the ravisher of his mistress, as the continent forgive the murders of Britain.

evidence.claim: The Almighty hath implanted in us these unextinguishable feelings for good and wise purposes.

candidate_claims.statement: The Almighty hath implanted in us these unextinguishable feelings for good and wise purposes.

evidence.claim: They are the guardians of his image in our hearts.

candidate_claims.statement: They are the guardians of his image in our hearts.

evidence.claim: They distinguish us from the herd of common animals.

candidate_claims.statement: They distinguish us from the herd of common animals.

evidence.claim: The social compact would dissolve, and justice be extirpated from the earth, or have only a casual existence were we callous to the touches of affection.

candidate_claims.statement: The social compact would dissolve, and justice be extirpated from the earth, or have only a casual existence were we callous to the touches of affection.

evidence.claim: The robber, and the murderer, would often escape unpunished, did not the injuries which our tempers sustain, provoke us into justice.

candidate_claims.statement: The robber, and the murderer, would often escape unpunished, did not the injuries which our tempers sustain, provoke us into justice.

evidence.claim: Ye that dare oppose, not only the tyranny, but the tyrant, stand forth!

candidate_claims.statement: Ye that dare oppose, not only the tyranny, but the tyrant, stand forth!

evidence.claim: Every spot of the old world is overrun with oppression.

candidate_claims.statement: Every spot of the old world is overrun with oppression.

candidate_claims.statement: Freedom hath been hunted round the globe.

evidence.claim: Asia, and Africa, have long expelled her.—Europe regards her like a stranger, and England hath given her warning to depart.

candidate_claims.statement: Asia, and Africa, have long expelled her.—Europe regards her like a stranger, and England hath given her warning to depart.

evidence.claim: receive the fugitive, and prepare in time an asylum for mankind.

candidate_claims.statement: receive the fugitive, and prepare in time an asylum for mankind.

candidate_claims.statement: Of the Present Ability of America: With Some Miscellaneous Reflections

evidence.claim: I have never met with a man, either in England or America, who hath not confessed his opinion, that a separation between the countries would take place one time or other: And there is no instance in which we have shown less judgment, than in endeavoring to describe, what we call, the ripeness or fitness of the continent for independence.

candidate_claims.statement: I have never met with a man, either in England or America, who hath not confessed his opinion, that a separation between the countries would take place one time or other: And there is no instance in which we have shown less judgment, than in endeavoring to describe, what we call, the ripeness or fitness of the continent for independence.

candidate_claims.statement: As all men allow the measure, and vary only in their opinion of the time, let us, in order to remove mistakes, take a general survey of things, and endeavor if possible to find out the _very_ time.

candidate_claims.statement: The general concurrence, the glorious union of all things, proves the fact.

evidence.claim: Nothing can be further from truth than this; for if America had only a twentieth part of the naval force of Britain, she would be by far an over-match for her; because, as we neither have, nor claim any foreign dominion, our whole force would be employed on our own coast where we should, in the long run, have two to one the advantage of those who had three or four thousand miles to sail over before they could attack us, and the same distance to return in order to refit and recruit.

evidence.claim: And although Britain, by her fleet, hath a check over our trade to Europe, we have as large a one over her trade to the West Indies, which, by laying in the neighborhood of the Continent, lies entirely at its mercy.

evidence.claim: If premiums were to be given to merchants to build and employ in their service ships mounted with twenty, thirty, forty, or fifty guns (the premiums to be in proportion to the loss of bulk to the merchant), fifty or sixty of those ships, with a few guardships on constant duty, would keep up a sufficient navy, and that without burdening ourselves with the evil so loudly complained of in England, of suffering their fleet in time of peace to lie rotting in the docks.

evidence.claim: — Were a manifesto to be published, and despatched to foreign Courts, setting forth the miseries we have endured, and the peaceful methods which we have ineffectually used for redress; declaring at the same time that not being able longer to live happily or safely under the cruel disposition of the British Court, we had been driven to the necessity of breaking off all connections with her; at the same time, assuring all such Courts of our peaceable disposition towards them, and of our desire of entering into trade with them; such a memorial would produce more good effects to this Continent than if a ship were freighted with petitions to Britain.

candidate_claims.statement: — Were a manifesto to be published, and despatched to foreign Courts, setting forth the miseries we have endured, and the peaceful methods which we have ineffectually used for redress; declaring at the same time that not being able longer to live happily or safely under the cruel disposition of the British Court, we had been driven to the necessity of breaking off all connections with her; at the same time, assuring all such Courts of our peaceable disposition towards them, and of our desire of entering into trade with them; such a memorial would produce more good effects to this Continent than if a ship were freighted with petitions to Britain.

evidence.claim: These proceedings may at first seem strange and difficult, but like all other steps which we have already passed over, will in a little time become familiar and agreeable; and until an independence is declared, the Continent will feel itself like a man who continues putting off some unpleasant business from day to day, yet knows it must be done, hates to set about it, wishes it over, and is continually haunted with the thoughts of its necessity.

evidence.claim: Sir John Dalrymple, the putative father of a whining jesuitical piece, fallaciously called, “The address of the people of England to the inhabitants of America,” hath perhaps from a vain supposition that the people here were to be frightened at the pomp and description of a king, given (though very unwisely on his part) the real character of the present one: “But,” says this writer, “if you are inclined to pay compliments to an administration, which we do not complain of (meaning the Marquis of Rockingham’s at the repeal of the Stamp Act) it is very unfair in you to withhold them from that prince, by whose NOD ALONE they were permitted to do any thing.” This is toryism with a witness!

evidence.claim: To be on the footing of 1763, it is not sufficient, that the laws only be put in the same state, but, that our circumstances likewise be put in the same state; our burnt and destroyed towns repaired or built up, our private losses made good, our public debts (contracted for defence) discharged; otherwise we shall be millions worse than we were at that enviable period.

candidate_claims.statement: A situation, similar to the present, hath not happened since the days of Noah until now.
